costs: State must commit multi-year staffing allowances even where enrollment is below optimal, creating a subsidy cost
Negotiation and contract-administration capacity requirements at county level
Risk of unequal uptake between well-organized and poorly organized municipalities
content.equity_impact: Could disproportionately benefit villages with strong local organizational capacity (mirroring the Erdőkürt social-capital pattern, F-live-portuguese_reform-10), leaving poorly organized or Roma-concentrated settlements without negotiating power unless a baseline entitlement is mandated centrally.
expected_benefits: Restores a formal, time-bound negotiation channel for municipalities without requiring full re-decentralization
Provides predictability (3-year staffing guarantee) that mitigates the zero-enrollment cliff dynamic in the interim
Leverages existing rural development funding architecture (Magyar Falu Program) already politically prioritized
content.goal: Re-introduce municipal co-responsibility and bargaining power over village-school viability through time-bound reciprocal contracts, replicating the French convention ruralité logic within Hungary's centralized maintenance framework.

implementation_steps.action: Design a pilot reciprocal-contract instrument for a sample of tankerületi districts with high closure risk
Negotiate 3-year contracts specifying staffing guarantees, transport co-financing, and shared-service commitments
Link existing rural development funding stream to co-financing obligations under the contracts
implementation_steps.timeline: ongoing, integrated with existing program from year 1

mechanisms: France's conventions ruralité establish reciprocal three-year contracts between the State and local elected officials where the State maintains posts while municipalities commit to network reorganization, with co-financing via equipment grants (F-live-international_comparison-03).

Hungary's 2013 centralization removed municipal bargaining power that such reciprocal arrangements presume, meaning any Hungarian version must be constructed as a state-initiated contractual instrument rather than a municipal right (F-live-international_comparison-04, F-live-portuguese_reform-01).

National class-size and per-pupil funding formulas create structural fiscal pressure independent of governance form, meaning the contract must include a funding-formula exception or guarantee to be effective (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04).
risks: Municipalities lack the fiscal capacity and legal standing that French communes have, so contracts may be asymmetric or purely symbolic
Contract enforcement is unclear given no precedent of binding state commitments at this scale (F-live-international_comparison-04)
Political sensitivity value if perceived as re-introducing municipal financial burden without matching devolved authority
content.title: Reciprocal rural-school contracts (convention-style) between state and municipalities
